title: Vehicle Trajectory Reconstruction Using Variational Inference Amid Measurement Loss and Non-Gaussian Noise: Algorithm and Experiments
authors: Jian Chen, Jiacheng He, Bei Peng, Qing Liang, Hongwei Wang, Mingzhu Wei
abstract: Trajectory reconstruction is essential for localizing and tracking maritime vehicles, but non-Gaussian process and measurement noises, as well as time-varying measurement loss, are present in the marine environment. To enhance TR accuracy in such challenging scenarios, this article proposes a state sequence estimation method based on the Rauch-Tung-Striebel smoother, which fully leverages the distributional characteristics of outliers. This approach models the likelihood density of non- Gaussian noise using two multivariate Laplace distributions. Unknown noise covariances are then modeled via an inverse Wishart distribution, while unknown measurement losses and their occurrence probabilities are addressed using Bernoulli and Beta distributions, respectively. On this basis, Variational Bayes inference is utilized to decouple fused anomalous processes, and infer states within a hierarchical model, yielding the proposed robust smoother. Experiments with an Unmanned Underwater Vehicle and numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of this proposed method for TR tasks.

title: Capacity Analysis and Robust Topology Design of LEO Satellite Constellation with Node/Link Failure
authors: Lei Guo, Junyu Liu, Min Sheng, Yang Li, Jiandong Li
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks are increasingly central to wide-area communication services, yet their capacity is highly sensitive to the inherent vulnerability of satellite and inter-satellite link (ISL) failures. This paper develops an analytical framework for characterizing network capacity under arbitrary failure patterns. Building on bisection cut-set analysis, we formalize capacity bottlenecks through graph partitioning and derive rigorous upper bounds on the network capacity. Within this framework, we further identify and characterize the maximum failure cut set, i.e., a critical subset of ISLs whose removal yields the most severe capacity degradation, and establish its necessity in achieving the worst-case capacity bound. Furthermore, the analytical framework has been shown to be applicable to general LEO networks, and a closed-form expression for capacity degradation has been derived using a 2D torus topology as an example, which takes into account both the number and spatial distribution of the satellite or ISL failure. The results indicate that maximizing the minimum bisection cut set capacity is fundamental to strengthening the intrinsic robustness of the LEO network. This paper provides a theoretical methodology for capacity assessment and offers principled guidelines for topology design in satellite constellations.

title:
Composition and Temperature Control of Spin Canting in Mn
                    <sub>3+x</sub>
                    Pt Noncollinear Antiferromagnet
authors: Dennis J. X. Lin, Bee Chun Lim, Jian Rui Soh, James Louremban, Hui Ru Tan, Hnin Yu Yu Ko, Mingsheng Zhang, Hang Khume Tan, Lisen Huang, Shaohai Chen, Pin Ho
abstract: Noncollinear antiferromagnetic (NCAF) materials such as L12-Mn3Pt are attractive for energy-efficient sensing, memory, and computing applications. Here, (001)-textured Mn3+xPt NCAF films are fabricated using a [Mn (tMn : 0.6 – 0.8 nm)/Pt 0.1 nm]30 multilayer structure, where the Mn thickness (tMn) is systematically modulated to control the composition and magnetic structure. Increasing the tMn adjusts the Mn:Pt ratio from 2.8:1 to 4.3:1, leading to the partial replacement of Pt lattice sites by the excess Mn atoms and thereby modifying the magnetic structure. The anomalous Hall effect loops of the multilayers exhibit polarity reversal at different critical transition temperature (Tcri), reducing from 235 to 135 K when tMn is increased from 0.6 to 0.7 nm. Meanwhile, the X-ray magnetic circular dichroism spectra of these multilayers exhibit similar temperature-driven polarity changes near Tcri. Collectively, these results indicate a composition- and temperature-controlled crossover from a predominantly NCAF to spin-canted NCAF order with ferromagnetic-like contribution.

title: Securing Blue Carbon: A Framework for Transparent MRV in Mangrove Restoration
authors: Devina Fitria Maharani, Andry Alamsyah, Herry Irawan
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Traditional Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) systems for blue carbon projects, particularly mangrove restoration, suffer from significant challenges. These systems are often centralized, costly, and inefficient, resulting in a lack of transparency, a high risk of data manipulation, and delays in carbon credit issuance. This paper proposes a novel blockchain-based MRV framework designed to address these critical flaws. A Proof-of-Concept (PoC) was developed that leverages a hybrid architecture, integrating Polygon for on-chain transactions, the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) for decentralized off-chain data storage, and registry rule-based Smart Contracts for automated verification. The framework is designed to manage credits as ERC-1155 tokens, which efficiently allows for the differentiation of credit vintages and project types on-chain. Functional validation of the PoC, conducted in a controlled EVM environment, demonstrated a 100% success rate across all on-chain stages, from data anchoring to VVB attestation. Performance analysis confirmed the economic viability of the design, with core operations, such as verification and anchoring, incurring low gas costs. The proposed framework establishes an immutable audit trail and enhances accountability through digital signatures, significantly improving the transparency, efficiency, and integrity of carbon credits for mangrove restoration projects. This work provides a critical foundation for scalable, high-integrity blue carbon markets by solving key data-handling and verification bottlenecks.

title: Machine Learning Comparison for Coffee Plantation Mapping in Jawa Barat’s Mountainous Terrain
authors: Anggun Tridawati, Ketut Wikantika, Trimuji Susantoro, Soni Darmawan, Muhammad Firman Ghazali, Ketut Tomy Suhari
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Coffee, a highly traded commodity, is cultivated globally in 70 tropical countries which has decreased from 2019 to 2020 on several continents. Therefore, coffee plantation yields must be monitored using remote sensing. Mapping in remote sensing generally can be done by using digital classification approaches. The choice of algorithm for classifying remote sensing data significantly impacts the accuracy. Random forest and support vector machine are two extensively used machine learning algorithms for mapping coffee plantations. Previous comparisons have shown inconsistent results, and no prior study has specifically compared RF and SVM for coffee plantation mapping. We used the Pansharp GeoEye-1, Digital Elevation Model, and Sentinel-2 (time series) to compare the performance of these two techniques for mapping coffee plantations. The results clearly show that the random forest algorithm outperforms support vector machine in terms of accuracy. The random forest algorithm obtained 79.33% overall accuracy and a kappa index of 0.77, whereas the support vector machine algorithm achieved 72.40% overall accuracy and a kappa value of 0.69. In terms of memory usage, during the classification process, random forest requires less storage space compared to support vector machine. Consequently, it can be concluded that the random forest algorithm is the superior choice for accurately mapping coffee plantations.

title: Small Spot Size and High Transmission Cold Cathode Electron Gun Based on Sharp Point Tungsten Emitter With Suppressor Electrode
authors: Wenrui Liang, Shuai Tang, Junhao Zhong, Caijian Zhang, Yan Shen, Yu Zhang, Shaozhi Deng
abstract: Cold cathode electron guns have demonstrated significant advantages of high resolution and high brightness in vacuum electronic devices. However, the tradeoff between electron transmittance and focal spot size (FSS) is a big challenge. This article proposes a scheme of using a sharp cathode and a suppressor electrode to achieve a small FSS and high transmittance in a cold cathode electron gun simultaneously. Through initial finite-element simulations of an electron beam, the electron gun with a sharp cathode shows much localized electric field and induces an original convergent electron beam. The electron gun with suppressor electrode reduces the electric field gradient on the cathode surface and generates a much focused beam combined with the gate electrode. The influence of the suppressor electrode and the cathode curvature radius on the electron transmittance and electron FSS is measured. The results show that after introducing the suppressor electrode structure and using a cathode with a smaller radius of curvature, the electron beam FSS has decreased by 53.8%, and the electron transmittance has increased by 12.2%. At present, the electron gun with a sharp tungsten field emitter of tip radius of 10 nm and suppressor electrode shows an electron beam FSS of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$88~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m and an electron transmittance of 42.7% under an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The results provide a feasible solution for achieving high electron transmission and small FSS of cold cathode guns.

title: Abnormal Degradations in a-InGaZnO TFTs Caused by Metal Reaction-Induced Doping under Self-Heating Stress
authors: Bo-Shen Huang, Ting-Chang Chang, Yu-An Chen, Ya-Ting Chien, Cheng-Ting Tsai, Tsung-Ming Tsai, Yang-Shun Fan, Chen-Shuo Huang, Chih-Hung Tsai
abstract: In this work, the abnormal degradation behaviors in amorphous InGaZnO (a-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) under self-heating stress (SHS) are discussed. Under SHS, it is observed that the threshold voltage (Vth) first shifts to the left and then to the right, accompanied by the appearance of a hump in the capacitance–voltage (C–V) measurement. During the subsequent recovery time, Vth continues to shift to the right, while the hump in the C–V measurement gradually disappears. Moreover, the results indicate that these degradation behaviors are related to both temperature and gate voltage. Among the observed degradations, the right shift of Vth is attributed to electron trapping into the gate insulator (GI), whereas the remaining phenomena originate from H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O molecules and their non-uniform distribution at the back interface of the channel, which become space charges under SHS. The presence of H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O molecules is due to insufficient protection by the SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> passivation layer, whereas their non-uniform distribution can be traced back to the metal reaction-induced doping process during device fabrication. The proposed model is further confirmed by extracting the length of the pre-accumulation region from normalized C–V curves under SHS with varying channel lengths.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Data-Driven Approaches in University Science Education: A Review of Applications and Outcomes
authors: Kadek Dwi Hendratma Gunawan, Jovita Ridhani, Annisa Nur Khasanah, Bayu Antrakusuma, Putu Harry Gunawan, Muhammad Nur Hudha
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and data-driven approaches into higher education has accelerated in recent years, particularly within science education, where traditional models often struggle with scalability, diverse learning needs, and assessment quality. This review examines applications of artificial intelligence (AI)—encompassing both classical machine-learning systems and emerging generative AI models—and data-driven methods in university-level science education. It uniquely contributes by mapping recent empirical evidence (2016–2025) through a governance–readiness– implementation lens that links technical deployment to pedagogical and ethical outcomes. A narrative review of peer-reviewed literature published between 2016 and 2025 was conducted, drawing on studies that explore the pedagogical, technological, and institutional dimensions of AI adoption. The findings indicate that AI is most commonly employed in learning analytics, predictive modeling, adaptive learning systems, and AI-enabled assessment and feedback, with additional applications in immersive laboratory environments and institutional decision-making. Across studies, these approaches often enhance personalization, support at-risk students, streamline faculty workload, and enable curriculum reform. Reported outcomes include improved academic performance, higher engagement, and greater student autonomy; however, these benefits are tempered by challenges relating to equity, data ethics, algorithmic bias, and sustainability. The review highlights that AI and data-driven education represent transformative yet ethically sensitive innovations whose effectiveness depends on governance frameworks, educator readiness, and responsible implementation. Universities are thus called to adopt AI not merely as a technical enhancement but as a pedagogical partner, ensuring its integration aligns with equity, integrity, and evidence-based learning. By synthesizing cross-disciplinary findings, this study extends prior reviews by delineating evidence strength and ethical governance mechanisms specific to STEM domains.

title: Systematic Literature Review - Prediction of Construction Project Delays Using the Random Forest Algorithm
authors: Ni Ketut Asriwinarti
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Construction project delays remain one of the most critical challenges in the global construction industry, often resulting in cost overruns, contractual conflicts, and reduced stakeholder satisfaction. Traditional approaches to delay analysis have shown limitations in handling complex and nonlinear project data, prompting increasing interest in the application of machine learning (ML) techniques for predictive analytics. This study aims to systematically review the application of the Random Forest (RF) algorithm in predicting construction project delays to provide a comprehensive understanding of its performance and research trends. Using the PRISMA methodology, 28 peer-reviewed articles published between 2023 and 2025 were identified and analyzed based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. The review addresses three main research questions: identifying key delay factors, understanding RF-based modeling approaches, and evaluating comparative algorithm performance. The findings reveal that the most common factors contributing to construction delays include activity duration, cost performance, contractor productivity, weather variability, and administrative efficiency. RF has been successfully utilized to capture nonlinear relationships and manage high-dimensional project data, achieving high prediction accuracy—often above 85%—in most studies. Compared to other ML models such as Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines, and Neural Networks, RF consistently demonstrates superior performance and robustness. Furthermore, hybrid RF models incorporating optimization techniques like Genetic Algorithms or feature-selection mechanisms show enhanced predictive accuracy and reduced error rates. This review contributes to advancing knowledge on data-driven delay prediction and reinforces the role of RF as a reliable and adaptable tool for proactive project risk management.

title: Compression-Oriented Video Super-Resolution
authors: Shuyun Wang, Yanbin Liu, Ming Lu, Zhuojie Wu, Senmao Tian, Yandong Guo, Xin Yu
abstract: Current compressed video super-resolution methods have achieved promising performance, but they often assume that an input video is compressed under low-delay configurations. However, under random access configurations, those methods might struggle to leverage the metadata effectively due to the large variations of metadata in different compression configurations. In this work, we propose a Compression-Oriented Video Super-Resolution (COVSR) method that can address video superresolution for both low-delay and random-access configurations. Specifically, we first introduce an efficient compression-aware propagation (ECAP) module that dynamically adjusts propagation routes in accordance with the compression configurations. Since existing methods require reconstructing frames in a frameby- frame manner, it is difficult to achieve efficient parallelization. However, we find that by slightly relaxing sequential dependencies, our ECAP can significantly improve inference speed. Furthermore, existing methods typically perform alignment between adjacent frames or adjacent features. However, since ECAP may propagate features along non-adjacent reference routes, it introduces new challenges for accurate cross-frame feature alignment. In response, we propose a metadata-driven alignment (MDA) module that refines cross-frame motion vectors into dense, feature-level flow offsets, enabling precise alignment across temporally distant features. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our COVSR not only achieves efficient and superior super-resolution performance but also is generalizable to various compression configurations. Our code will be available and the project page is at https://covsr.github.io.

title: Mode Interference Effect of a High-Frequency Linear Antenna in an Ionospheric Plasma
authors: Xue Wei Zhang, Nan Nan Sun, Li Na He, Tong He, Kai Li
abstract: With the development of satellite launching technology, spaceborne high-frequency (HF) linear antennas are being widely used in fields such as Earth observation and ionospheric sounding. In this article, we carry out an in-depth study on the effects of mode interference for an HF linear antenna immersed in a homogeneous anisotropic ionospheric plasma. Closed-form expressions for the radiated field by a finite-length linear antenna with nonuniform current distribution are derived analytically. The results show that HF linear antennas operating in the ionospheric plasma would exhibit disparate radiation characteristics from those in free space, where the ordinary wave and extraordinary wave contribute to the antenna’s current distribution and radiation pattern simultaneously and induce interferences. Pronounced minima at certain intervals of propagation distances in the total field are observed under substantial interference between the two waves. Additionally, the field pattern of each eigenmode varies slowly with the radiation direction, while the synthetic field is greatly affected by mode interference effects in both the near-zone and far-zone. This work may provide a theoretical foundation and guidance to the design and optimization of practical spaceborne HF antenna applications.

title: Relative hulls and their variations of narrow-sense primitive BCH codes
authors: Chunyu Gan, Chengju Li, Sihem Mesnager
abstract: The relative hull of a linear code C1 with respect to another linear code C2 is defined as the intersection of C1 and the dual of C2, i.e., C1 ∩ C⊥2. Anderson et al. [2] demonstrated that the dimension of the relative hull can be adjusted, either repeatedly increased or decreased by one, until a certain bound is reached by substituting either C1 or C2 with its equivalent code. As a special class of linear codes, BCH codes are both theoretically significant and practically valuable for communication and storage systems due to their good algebraic structures and flexible error-correcting capabilities. This raises an important question: how does the dimension of the relative hull change when C1 and C2 are BCH codes or their equivalent codes? This paper focuses on the relative hull dimensions of narrow-sense primitive BCH codes, building on the insights from [2]. We present several sufficient and necessary conditions based on the designed distances of BCH codes, which ensure that the dimensions of the relative hulls reach the lower or upper bounds for linear codes. Additionally, we study the parameters of the relative hulls of various classes of BCH codes, providing details on their dimensions and developing lower bounds for their minimum distances. Furthermore, we investigate how the dimensions of the relative hulls are affected when the BCH codes C2 are replaced by their equivalent codes C′2.

title: Design and Development of PaDyViR (Particle Dynamics Virtual Reality): From Storyboard to Prototype
authors: Supurwoko Supurwoko, Kadek Dwi Hendratma Gunawan, Muhammad Nur Hudha
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Physics education often struggles to convey abstract concepts such as force, mass, acceleration, friction, and collisions, which students frequently find difficult to visualize. Virtual reality (VR) offers promising opportunities to address this challenge by providing embodied, interactive representations of otherwise invisible physical phenomena. This study aims to describe the design and development of PaDyViR (Particle Dynamics Virtual Reality), a VR module created to support the teaching of particle dynamics through a storyboard-driven instructional design process. Employing a Research and Development (R&D) approach based on the 4D model (Define, Design, Develop, and Disseminate), the study focused on the first three stages. Activities included needs analysis, storyboard creation and validation, and prototype development using Blender for 3D modeling and Unity’s physics engine for realistic simulations. The results yielded a functional VR prototype aligned with Newtonian mechanics, consisting of sequential learning scenarios on force and acceleration, static and kinetic friction, and elastic collisions. Screenshots documented the transition from storyboard to immersive interface, demonstrating fidelity to curricular standards and expert validation. The findings indicate that PaDyViR is pedagogically coherent, technically stable, and ready for future empirical validation. Subsequent research should evaluate its usability, effectiveness, and impact on learning outcomes.

title: Why Shorter Time Steps Induce the Failure Probability Curse in Power Outage Risk Analysis: An Explanation and Solution
authors: Jiawei Wang, Qinglai Guo, Hongbin Sun
abstract: Recent climate change has led to more frequent and widespread extreme weather events, causing damage to power system components and resulting in significant outages and losses. Component fragility curves and sequential Monte Carlo simulations have been widely used for power system outage risk assessment. Fragility curves serve as the bridge linking weather events to component failures, while sequential Monte Carlo methods capture the temporal dynamics of weather and balance probabilities with consequences. However, a direct and improper combination of these two tools can introduce significant bias, especially under high time resolution. This phenomenon is referred to as the “failure probability curse.” This paper analyzes the fundamental cause of this issue and proposes a conditional-probability correction method for the sequential Monte Carlo simulation. The method can eliminate the inherent bias from high-time-resolution sampling in power outage risk analysis and reconcile sequential Monte Carlo with different physical failure mechanisms. Furthermore, the conditional-probability perspective provided in this paper offers support and explanation for related existing methods at the level of mathematical principles, enhancing the insights of the “failure probability curse” from both an explanation and solution perspective. Numerical experiments based on an illustrative system and the Tennessee power network confirm the validity of the proposed approach.

title: An Unsupervised Image Dehazing with Scene Geometry Prior for Road Traffic Scenarios
authors: Mingye Ju, Tianyi Lyu, Chunming He, Qingshan Liu, Kai-Kuang Ma
abstract: Despite advances in single image dehazing, robust dehazing for real-world road traffic scenes remains challenging due to scarce paired data, traffic-specific geometry, and real-time constraints. To address this issue, we propose a novel image prior for road traffic scenes, termed scene geometry prior (SGP), which leverages depth cues derived from vanishing point (VP) to provide geometry-aware guidance and reduce reliance on paired training data. Our SGP comprises two components: a global SGP (G-SGP) that captures the global geometric distribution and a non-local SGP (NL-SGP) that corrects the errors, among obstructions belonging to the same category, in captured global distribution. Building on the proposed prior, we develop a lightweight and unsupervised road traffic image dehazing network (RTDnet). It consists of a main sub-network guided by the G-SGP to reconstruct the haze-free image, alongside two auxiliary sub-networks that leverage the NL-SGP and VP information to respectively estimate transmission map, and atmospheric light. During training, we introduce an atmospheric scattering model (ASM)-driven mutual-boost learning mechanism (ASM-ML), which is rooted in Bayesian theory and effectively integrates the strengths of different priors without mutual interference while distilling ASM-based physical knowledge into each subnetwork. By coupling SGP with ASM-ML, RTDnet can be trained without paired traffic data by exploiting traffic-specific geometry, whose accurate guidance reduces the reliance on large model capacity and enables lightweight real-time deployment. Experiments demonstrate that our RTDnet surpasses state-of-the-art competitors in terms of restoration quality, efficiency, and model size. Moreover, its robust dehazing performance benefits downstream tasks operating in hazy conditions.

                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Plasma Pretreatment: Effects on the Interface Properties in Si
                    <sub>3</sub>

authors: Jiaofen Yang, Ming Tao, Jing Xiao, Kai Tang, Yilan Chen, Wenjing Zhang, Bin Zhang, Bowen Huang, Jie Liu, Hongyue Wang, Jinyan Wang, Maojun Wang
abstract: This work investigates the effects of N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> plasma pretreatment (PP) on the gate interface formation and interface defects in Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>/AlN/GaN metal–insulator–semiconductor high-electron-mobility transistors (MISHEMTs) through first-principles calculations. Based on STEM, EDX, and EELS, the interface structures with various O/N ratios are constructed to simulate no PP, N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP, and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP on the etched GaN surface. The GaN surface with no PP or N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP exhibits a high O/N ratio exceeding 66.7%, facilitating the growth of N-polar AlN. This AlN layer is almost entirely oxidized, ultimately forming an Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interface. In contrast, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP achieves the GaN surface with a low O/N ratio below 66.7%, promoting the growth of Al-polar AlN, which exhibits significantly lower oxidation. Consequently, the Al-polar-AlN/GaN and Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interfaces are ultimately formed. For the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/GaN interfaces formed in different PP processes, compared to no PP, both N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP and H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP can reduce Ga, O, and Al dangling bonds (DBs) but increase N DBs, thereby suppressing interface defects to some extent. However, an almost defect-free high-quality AlN/GaN interface is formed in H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP, thereby further significantly reducing these DBs and demonstrating the best interface performance. Experimental and calculated results confirm that all these interface defects are acceptors, which trap electrons and cause a positive threshold voltage shift. Therefore, H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP achieves the lowest density of interface acceptor defects, culminating in fabricated devices with superior threshold voltage stability compared to those with N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> PP.

title: High-Performance Flexible ITZO-Based Deep-Ultraviolet Phototransistors for Retina-Inspired Vision Sensors
authors: Delang Lin, Guijun Li, Man Chun Tseng, Fion Sze Yan Yeung, Hoi Sing Kwok, Rongsheng Chen
abstract: Retina-inspired vision sensors can extract input visual information through preprocessing operations to reduce system power consumption and latency. Metal–oxide thin-film transistor (MOTFT)-based phototransistors are attractive for in-sensor computing, but are often limited by sluggish temporal response, constraining high-frequency and time-dependent operation. To address this constraint, a flexible InSnMgO (ITMO)/InSnZnO (ITZO)/ITMO heterojunction deep-ultraviolet phototransistor (HDPT) was developed as an active vision sensor that exhibited stable photosynaptic behavior and emulated retinal light adaptation. Under 5-ms deep-ultraviolet (DUV) pulses, the device sustained 100-Hz operation with the 26-fJ/spike single-event energy and enabled 4-bit temporal sequence recognition. By combining blockwise superpixel aggregation and the HDPT-enabled 4-bit temporal encoding, the network input dimension was reduced to 25% of the original size while achieving over 95% accuracy in static image recognition. For dynamic perception, a 95% accuracy was achieved on dynamic gesture recognition. These results indicate hardware-level feature encoding and dimensionality reduction at the point of capture, enabling low-power, dynamic in-sensor vision.

authors: Laishram Premjit Singh, Romesh Laishram, Sapam Bikesh, Bimoljit Chanam, Trupti Ranjan Lenka, Tanjim Rahman, Hieu P. Nguyen, Biraj Shougaijam
abstract: In this work, Ag-nanoparticles were deposited on TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-thin film (Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF) over the p-type Si (100) substrate through the simple glancing angle deposition (GLAD) method for advanced photodetector applications. The FE-SEM analysis manifests that the Ag nanoparticles (NPs) were successfully fabricated over the surface of the TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> thin films (TFs). Furthermore, the XRD analysis confirmed the presence of a pure polycrystalline nature of anatase TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and Ag. The Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> TF sample exhibits enhanced photon absorption as compared to the bare TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF sample in the ultraviolet (UV) and visible region. Under the illumination of 390 nm, the Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based photodetector device gives excellent performance metrics such as responsivity (R), external quantum efficiency (EQE), detectivity (D), and noise equivalent power (NEP) of ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ ~74.25$</tex-math> </inline-formula> A/W, ~236.08%, ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 2.74\times 10^{{13}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> Jones, and ~<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ 5.84\times 10^{-{12}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> W, respectively, at +3 V. Furthermore, the photosensitivity of ~3.36 and rise/fall times of ~98/95 ms are obtained from Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based device. Moreover, the Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF-based device gives good responsivity values of ~27.48 and 11.53 A/W under 532 nm (green) and 685 nm (red), respectively. These results confirm that photodetectors based on Ag-NPs/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-TF fabricated using the GLAD technique can be suitable for broadband photodetector applications.

title: Dynamically Continuous Tunable Metasurface With Switchable Transmission and Absorption Modes
authors: Zhe Qin, Kaihao Zhou, Yongfeng Li, Jiechu Liu, Lixin Jiang, Wanwan Yang, Hao Yang, Zhihao Guo, Lin Zheng, Hongya Chen, Wenjie Wang, Jiafu Wang, Hua Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: The control of electromagnetic (EM) waves, especially over transmission, reflection, and absorption, is a cornerstone for advanced EM systems such as smart radomes and adaptive camouflage. However, achieving continuous control over these three responses within the same frequency band remains a significant challenge for active metasurfaces. Existing designs often suffer from discrete switching, narrow bandwidth, high insertion loss, and operating in separate bands. Therefore, this article proposes a novel active metasurface capable of dynamically and continuously regulating transmission and absorption responses in the same operational band. The metasurface comprises two key modules: an active frequency-selective layer and an active absorption layer. By biasing p-i-n diodes in their nonlinear region, the equivalent impedance of the absorption layer can be adjusted, enabling precise control over both the transmission loss and the ohmic loss. In the transmission-continuous-modulation mode, the EM response transitions from near-unity transmission to high reflection; in the absorption-continuous-modulation mode, transmission is suppressed, and reflection is tuned from perfect reflection to near-total absorption. Both simulations and measurements validate the metasurface’s performance, showing a wide −1dB transmission band and a broad −10 dB absorption band, with continuous tunability under bias voltage control. The proposed strategy offers a unified, low-profile, and versatile solution for developing next-generation adaptive EM systems.

title: Background-Oriented Schlieren Velocimetry of Helium Coolant Flow in Additively Manufactured Channels
authors: Cody S. Wiggins, Yuqiao Fan, Chris Crawford
abstract: High-pressure helium gas cooling is an attractive solution for thermal management of the fusion blanket first wall, as this coolant is chemically and neutronically inert and separable from hydrogenic species. However, due to the low thermal mass of helium, geometric optimization of these channels is required to provide sufficient cooling at manageable flow rates and pumping burdens. Increasingly, analysis and optimization of these coolant channels rely on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, and these require relevant experimental data for turbulence model validation. Toward this end, a high-pressure helium gas flow visualization system has been employed to image the flow of helium in flow channels with one-sided heating, mimicking the blanket first wall environment. Flow of helium at 4 MPa pressure and flow rates up to 68 g/s (Reynolds number 57 000) is supplied to rectangular channel test sections, with uniform heating applied to the bottom wall of the channel at heat fluxes varied between roughly 50 and 130 kW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. A high-speed camera is used to image index of refraction (IOR) gradients in the fluid via background-oriented schlieren (BOS), and temperature and pressure instrumentation are used to characterize thermal-hydraulic performance of each channel. Cross correlation of time-resolved BOS images is then used to calculate time-averaged 2-D helium velocity fields. Flow in additively manufactured (AM) channels is examined in this manner, including both featureless channels and those containing baffling as a heat transfer enhancement. The flow distribution seen in the featureless case differs significantly from that seen in prior simulations, whereas the flow in the baffled case shows the predicted behavior of flow forced along the heated wall. This augmented flow distribution is seen to increase the heat transfer coefficient in the baffled test section. Strategies are discussed for ongoing and future validation of these simulations, with the aim of model deployment for blanket cooling design and optimization.

title: BC-AEAD: Blockchain-enabled Lightweight AEAD Combiner for IoT Device Authentication
authors: Aswani Devi Aguru, Suresh Babu Erukala
abstract: The enormous proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) networks necessitates robust and ultra-lightweight authentication mechanisms. Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) schemes are pivotal for resource-constrained devices, but centralized models suffer from a single point of failure. We propose BC-AEAD, a Blockchain-enabled IoT authentication and Authenticated Key Establishment (AKE) scheme using a novel AEAD combiner. BC-AEAD strategically amalgamates the high-performance tweakable block cipher primitive of LOCUS-AEAD with the resource-efficient sponge construction of ASCON. Leveraging Blockchain enables decentralized and tamper-proof key management of the AEAD combiner. The robustness of proposed combiner is validated using an informal security analysis, the Real-and-Random Oracle Model (ROR), and the Scyther tool. The performance of Blockchain network is evaluated regarding throughput, latency, and resource consumption using Hyperledger Caliper v0.4.2. The comparison of computation and communication costs of AKE schemes with state-of-the-art works is accomplished using the Pycrypto toolkit. The cost analysis of BC-AEAD on FPGA proves its practical deployability on highly constrained IoT devices with 1146 LUTs, 428 slices, and 1.86 Gbps. Eventually, the lack of a direct precedent strongly evaluates the core novelty of our unique AEAD combiner.

title: Integrating Physiological and Metabolic Indicators for Heart Disease Risk Prediction Using Machine Learning Approaches
authors: I Gede Septa Adi Gunajaya, Ni Gede Dwita Putri Shanti, I Wayan Galang Utama Giri, I Gede Abi Bhakti Paramartha, Gde Palguna Reganata
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Geoinformatics (ICDSG)
abstract: Heart disease remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, emphasizing the need for innovative and non-invasive screening approaches for early detection. Fatigue, a multidimensional physiological and psychological state, has been linked to autonomic nervous system imbalance and cardiovascular risk. Pupil diameter reflects sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, making pupil changes a promising biomarker for assessing fatigue and related cardiovascular conditions. This study aimed to develop a machine learning–based screening model integrating pupil response, fatigue indicators, and metabolic parameters to predict early risk of heart disease. Data was obtained from experimental pupillary measurements and public clinical datasets, including blood pressure, cholesterol, and glucose levels. Logistic Regression, Artificial Neural Network (ANN), and XGBoost algorithms were applied to construct screening models, with ensemble soft voting used for performance optimization. The integrated model achieved good predictive accuracy (AUC 0.79) in distinguishing high-and low-risk categories, demonstrating that pupil-based physiological features can complement traditional risk markers. Pupil changes exhibit dilation in individuals with moderate fatigue and shift toward pupil contriction in those with high fatigue which is related with cardiovascular risk. The findings suggest that the screening model provides a non-invasive, data-driven screening approach for early identification of individuals at potential cardiovascular risk. This approach may enhance preventive strategies and facilitate personalized health monitoring using accessible physiological indicators.
